slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's time to become box plot artists! You'll transform numbers into visual stories that reveal the personality of each sport's data. Each box plot is like a unique fingerprint of the data!

items: What do the different parts of a box plot represent?
How do you identify an outlier using the IQR method?
When would a box plot be more useful than a histogram for displaying data?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's make sure you've mastered these box plot skills! These questions will help us review the key concepts and make sure you're ready to use box plots like a pro statistician.

slides.text: Reflect on how box plots help us understand data distribution and compare different data sets. Discuss real-world applications of box plots in sports analysis, business, and science.
slides.mastery_check: Student can create a complete box plot from a data set, identify outliers using the 1.5 × IQR rule, and explain what the box plot reveals about the data distribution.
slides.mascot_explanation: Fantastic work today, statisticians! You've learned to create powerful visual tools that help people understand data at a glance. Box plots are used everywhere from sports analysis to medical research - you now have a skill that connects to the real world!

title: Abstract Expressionism Self-Portrait: Conveying Emotion Through Color and Gesture
description: Students explore Abstract Expressionism by creating self-portraits that focus on emotional expression rather than realistic representation. They learn about artists like Jackson Pollock and Helen Frankenthaler while experimenting with gestural painting techniques.
instructions: Introduce Abstract Expressionism and key artists, focusing on how they used color and gesture to express emotions rather than realistic forms.
Discuss the difference between representational and abstract art, showing examples of traditional vs. abstract self-portraits.
Have students reflect on their current emotions and choose colors that represent those feelings.
Demonstrate gestural painting techniques including dripping, splattering, and bold brushstrokes.
Guide students in creating abstract self-portraits using these techniques, focusing on emotional expression over realistic features.
Encourage experimentation with layering colors and varying brushstroke intensity to convey different emotions.
Have students share their work and explain the emotions and techniques they used.
Reflect on how abstract art can sometimes communicate feelings more powerfully than realistic art.
parent_notes: This lesson embodies the Montessori principle of allowing children to express their authentic selves while learning about cultural heritage through art history. Abstract Expressionism provides a perfect vehicle for emotional expression without the pressure of technical perfection, encouraging creative risk-taking and personal voice. The gestural nature of this art form allows for kinesthetic engagement with materials. Support your child's artistic exploration by focusing on their process and emotional expression rather than the final product, and encourage them to continue experimenting with abstract techniques in future art-making.

slides.heading: Abstract Expressionism Self-Portrait
slides.subheading: Conveying Emotion Through Color and Gesture
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, young artists! I'm Finn Fox, and today we're going to explore a revolutionary art movement called Abstract Expressionism. Instead of painting what things look like, we'll paint how things feel - it's like translating emotions into colors and movements!

items: Acrylic or tempera paints
Large paper or canvas boards
Various brushes and sponges
Cups for water
Paper plates for palettes
Aprons or old clothes
slides.setup_instructions: Set up your painting area with protective covering. Arrange paints and brushes within easy reach. Have water cups ready for cleaning brushes between colors.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's prepare our artist studio! Abstract Expressionism can get wonderfully messy as we let our emotions flow through the paint, so make sure you're dressed for creative adventure. This is going to be so much fun!

slides.text: Learn about Abstract Expressionism and artists like Jackson Pollock and Helen Frankenthaler. Observe how they used color, gesture, and texture to express emotions rather than realistic forms.
slides.mascot_explanation: These amazing artists discovered that you don't need to paint exactly what you see to create powerful art! They painted their feelings, their energy, and their inner experiences - just like we're going to do today.

slides.text: Reflect on your current emotions and choose a color palette that represents those feelings. Consider warm colors for energetic emotions and cool colors for calm or sad feelings.
slides.mascot_explanation: Take a moment to check in with yourself - how are you feeling right now? Excited? Peaceful? Maybe a mix of different emotions? Let's choose colors that match your inner emotional landscape!

slides.prompt: Create an abstract self-portrait using gestural painting techniques. Focus on expressing your emotions through bold brushstrokes, color choices, and paint application rather than realistic facial features.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now for the magical part - let your emotions guide your brush! Don't worry about making it look like a traditional portrait. Instead, let your feelings dance across the paper through color and movement. There's no wrong way to do this!

items: How is Abstract Expressionism different from realistic art?
What techniques did you use to express emotion in your self-portrait?
How do color choices affect the mood of an artwork?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's reflect on this amazing artistic journey! These questions will help us think about what we've learned about expressing ourselves through abstract art and how colors and gestures can communicate feelings.

slides.text: Share your abstract self-portraits and explain the emotions and techniques you used. Discuss how abstract art can sometimes communicate feelings more powerfully than realistic representation.
slides.mastery_check: Student can identify characteristics of Abstract Expressionism, use gestural techniques to express emotion, and explain their artistic choices in relation to emotional expression.
slides.mascot_explanation: What incredible abstract self-portraits you've created! You've learned to speak the language of emotions through art, just like the great Abstract Expressionist masters. Remember, art isn't just about copying what we see - it's about sharing what we feel!

title: Brown Girl Dreaming: Poetry as Personal History and Identity Expression
description: Students explore how Jacqueline Woodson uses poetry to tell her personal story and express her developing identity. They analyze specific poems from the memoir and discuss how poetry can capture memories and emotions differently than prose.
instructions: Read selected poems from Brown Girl Dreaming that focus on identity, family, and belonging.
Discuss how Woodson uses specific poetic devices like line breaks, imagery, and rhythm to convey meaning and emotion.
Explore the themes of identity formation, family traditions, and finding one's place in the world.
Have students identify moments where Woodson's identity is shaped by her experiences, both positive and challenging.
Discuss the historical context of the Civil Rights era and how it affects the author's childhood experiences.
Compare poetry memoir format to traditional prose memoirs - what are the advantages of each?
Encourage students to think about their own identity-forming moments and how they might express them poetically.
Reflect on how personal stories connect to larger historical and cultural narratives.
materials_needed: Copy of Brown Girl Dreaming by Jacqueline Woodson
parent_notes: This lesson exemplifies the Montessori approach of honoring the child's developing sense of self while connecting personal experience to broader human experience. Woodson's memoir validates the importance of each child's unique story while introducing complex themes of identity, race, and belonging in age-appropriate ways. The poetry format makes these themes accessible while developing literary appreciation. Encourage your child to share their own family stories and traditions, helping them see the value in their personal narrative. This reading supports empathy development and cultural understanding essential for global citizenship.

slides.heading: Brown Girl Dreaming
slides.subheading: Poetry as Personal History and Identity Expression
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, story explorers! I'm Finn Fox, and today we're diving into Jacqueline Woodson's beautiful memoir written in poems. We'll discover how poetry can tell life stories in ways that touch our hearts and help us understand identity and belonging.

items: Copy of Brown Girl Dreaming by Jacqueline Woodson
Notebook for responses
Poetry analysis worksheet
Markers or colored pencils
Historical context handout about 1960s-70s
slides.setup_instructions: Find a comfortable reading spot where you can focus on the poetry and take thoughtful notes. Have your materials organized for both reading and reflecting.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's set up our poetry exploration station! These materials will help us dig deep into Woodson's beautiful words and understand how she uses poetry to share her life story. I'm excited to discover these poems with you!

slides.text: Read selected poems focusing on family, identity, and belonging. Pay attention to how Woodson describes her experiences growing up and the people who shaped her.
slides.mascot_explanation: As we read these poems, listen for the music in Woodson's words and notice how she paints pictures of her childhood with language. Each poem is like a memory captured in verse!

slides.text: Analyze Woodson's use of poetic devices like line breaks, imagery, and repetition. Discuss how these techniques help convey emotions and create meaning beyond just the words themselves.
slides.mascot_explanation: Poets have special tools just like artists have brushes and paint! Let's explore how Woodson uses the shape and sound of her poems to make us feel what she felt as a young girl growing up.

slides.prompt: Create a visual response to one of the poems by illustrating key images or emotions, then write about a moment from your own life that connects to Woodson's experiences of identity and belonging.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be creative! You'll make art inspired by Woodson's poems and then think about your own story. What moments in your life have helped shape who you are becoming?

items: How does Woodson use poetry to express her developing sense of identity?
What historical events influenced the author's childhood experiences?
How is reading a memoir in poetry different from reading one written in regular paragraphs?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's check our understanding of this beautiful memoir! These questions will help us think about how Woodson uses poetry as a powerful way to share personal history and explore identity.

slides.text: Reflect on how personal stories like Woodson's help us understand both individual identity formation and broader historical experiences. Discuss the power of poetry to capture memories and emotions.
slides.mastery_check: Student can identify how Woodson uses poetic techniques to express identity themes, connect the memoir to historical context, and articulate how poetry serves as an effective memoir format.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a beautiful exploration of poetry and personal history! You've discovered how writers like Jacqueline Woodson use the power of poetry to share their life stories and help us all understand the journey of growing up and finding our place in the world.

title: Global Youth Movements: Young Activists Shaping Environmental Change
description: Students explore youth-led environmental movements around the world, examining how young people are advocating for climate action and sustainability. They analyze different approaches to activism and consider their own potential roles in environmental stewardship.
instructions: Introduce the concept of youth activism and its historical significance in social change movements.
Research prominent young environmental activists from different continents (Greta Thunberg, Autumn Peltier, Mari Copeny, etc.).
Examine different forms of environmental activism: protests, education, innovation, policy advocacy, and community organizing.
Discuss the unique perspective and power that young people bring to environmental issues.
Explore local environmental challenges that could benefit from youth involvement and action.
Have students identify environmental issues they care about and brainstorm age-appropriate action steps.
Connect youth environmental activism to the broader theme of global citizenship and responsibility.
Reflect on how young people can make meaningful contributions to environmental solutions.
materials_needed: Photos and articles about young environmental activists
World map for marking activist locations
Large paper for creating action plans
parent_notes: This lesson embodies the Montessori philosophy of empowering children to see themselves as agents of positive change in the world. By studying youth activists, students learn that their voices and actions matter, regardless of age. The focus on environmental stewardship connects to Montessori's emphasis on cosmic education and care for the Earth. This lesson builds global citizenship awareness while encouraging local action. Support your child's developing sense of agency by discussing environmental issues together and identifying ways your family can contribute to sustainability efforts. Emphasize that meaningful change often starts with individual commitment and spreads through community action.

slides.heading: Global Youth Movements
slides.subheading: Young Activists Shaping Environmental Change
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, future changemakers! I'm Finn Fox, and today we're going to learn about amazing young people around the world who are fighting to protect our planet. These youth activists prove that you're never too young to make a difference!

items: Photos and articles about young environmental activists
World map for marking activist locations
Research worksheets
Large paper for creating action plans
Colored markers
slides.setup_instructions: Organize materials for research and mapping activities. Have the world map ready to mark locations of different youth activists as we learn about them.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's gather our materials to explore the inspiring world of youth environmental activism! We'll travel around the globe learning about brave young people who are standing up for our planet's future.

slides.text: Learn about prominent young environmental activists from different continents. Research their backgrounds, the issues they address, and their methods of creating change.
slides.mascot_explanation: We're going to meet some incredible young heroes! From Greta Thunberg in Sweden to Autumn Peltier in Canada, these activists show us that young voices can be powerful forces for environmental protection.

slides.text: Examine different forms of environmental activism including protests, education campaigns, innovative solutions, policy advocacy, and community organizing. Discuss the strengths of each approach.
slides.mascot_explanation: Just like there are many ways to help a friend, there are many ways to help our planet! Some activists march and protest, others teach and educate, and some invent new solutions - let's explore all these different paths to change.

slides.prompt: Choose a local environmental issue and create an action plan that incorporates youth perspectives and age-appropriate activism strategies. Present your plan to the group.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be environmental leaders! Think about an environmental challenge in your community and design a plan that shows how young people like you can make a real difference. Remember, every big change starts with someone caring enough to take the first step!

items: What unique advantages do young people have as environmental activists?
Name three different approaches to environmental activism and give an example of each.
How can youth environmental movements create lasting change in their communities?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's make sure we understand the power of youth environmental activism! These questions will help us think about why young voices are so important in protecting our planet and how you can be part of the solution.

slides.text: Reflect on the global impact of youth environmental movements and consider ways to get involved locally. Discuss how young activists inspire hope and demonstrate that age doesn't limit one's ability to create positive change.
slides.mastery_check: Student can identify key youth environmental activists and their methods, explain different forms of activism, and articulate how young people can contribute to environmental solutions.
slides.mascot_explanation: What an inspiring journey through global youth activism! You've learned that young people like you are already changing the world and protecting our planet. Remember, you have the power to make a difference - your voice, your ideas, and your actions matter more than you might think!
